coredata.dc:title: Iron ion detection and life cycle assessment of cutting fluid based on photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Lian, Jiadi
Chen, Bifeng
Han, Qing
Xie, Hangqing
Xu, Jing
coredata.dc:description: Cutting fluid (CF) ensures machining accuracy and improves tool life in machining, which is of great significance for its impurity content detection and life cycle evaluation. In this paper, citric acid and arginine were used as carbon and nitrogen sources, photoluminescent (PL) nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) with a particle size ranging from 8 to 14 nm and well dispersion in water-based CF were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal reaction method. The N-CDs have the best signal under 360 nm ultraviolet light excitation, which well avoids the autofluorescence of the CF. The N-CDs dispersion have good selectivity and linearity in the Fe3+ concentration range of 2 to 80 μM, and the detection limit is 1.2 μM. The N-CDs were used to detect the concentration of Fe3+ in CF with different working hours, and the results were consistent with the results of coupled plasma emission spectroscopy. This study provides an intelligent sensing platform for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ in the CF environment, and provides a promising strategy for the evaluation of the CF lifetime.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cutting fluid
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Iron ion detection
Iife cycle assessment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interaction between Fe3+ and the N-CDs
Detection of Fe3+ in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research is financially supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 52275182 , No. 12304459 ), the National Nature Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province, China (No. LTGS23E060002 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang , China (No. GK229909299001-14 ), the Tribology Science Fund of State Key Laboratory of tribology in Advanced Equipment (No. SKLTKF22B02 ), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. LQ21E030005 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang (No. 2021YW44 ).

The research is financially supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No.52275182, No.12304459), the National Nature Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province, China (No. LTGS23E060002), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang, China (No. GK229909299001-14), the Tribology Science Fund of State Key Laboratory of tribology in Advanced Equipment (No.SKLTKF22B02), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No.LQ21E030005) and the Fcundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang (No.2021YW44).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the N-CDs (scale bar = 100 nm) with the inset showing the size distribution of the N-CDs. (b) FTIR spectrum of the N-CDs. (c) XPS spectrum of the N-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (d), N1s (e), and O1s (f) of the N-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs with and without Fe3+. (b) PL and PLE spectra of N-CDs with and without Fe3+. (c) FTIR spectra of N-CDs, red, blue and yellow line are N-CDs: Fe3+ =1:0, 1:0.1 and 1:1. (d) Time resolved PL of N-CDs and N-CDs with 40 μM Fe3+.

(a) Comparison of the autofluorescence of CF and the PL spectra of N-CDs. (b) PL spectra of the N-CDs in CF at various excitation wavelengths. (c) PL spectra of the N-CDs with different concentrations of Fe3+. (d) Linear relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and Fe3+ concentration. (The blue dot: CF of 150 and 600 h for machining iron workpieces.) (e) Effect of interfering substances on the PL intensity of N-CDs. (The orange column: Add interfering ions to N-CDs. The green column: A further 40 μM Fe3+ was added to the N-CDs containing interfering ions.).
PL quenching mechanism of N-CDs by Fe3+.
Comparison of different CDs probe for the detection of Fe3+.

ce:simple-para.ce:bold: detection in CF and CF in certain working hours.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: 150 h and 600 h represent the processing hours of CF in the machine tool.

head.ce:title: Iron ion detection and life cycle assessment of cutting fluid based on photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering

Institute of Optoelectronic Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Optoelectronic Materials and Devices of Zhejiang Province, Institute of Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, China Jiliang University, Hang Zhou 310018, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hang Zhou 310018, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hang Zhou 310018, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cutting fluid (CF) ensures machining accuracy and improves tool life in machining, which is of great significance for its impurity content detection and life cycle evaluation. In this paper, citric acid and arginine were used as carbon and nitrogen sources, photoluminescent (PL) nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) with a particle size ranging from 8 to 14 nm and well dispersion in water-based CF were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal reaction method. The N-CDs have the best signal under 360 nm ultraviolet light excitation, which well avoids the autofluorescence of the CF. The N-CDs dispersion have good selectivity and linearity in the Fe3+ concentration range of 2 to 80 μM, and the detection limit is 1.2 μM. The N-CDs were used to detect the concentration of Fe3+ in CF with different working hours, and the results were consistent with the results of coupled plasma emission spectroscopy. This study provides an intelligent sensing platform for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ in the CF environment, and provides a promising strategy for the evaluation of the CF lifetime.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cooling and lubricating effect of cutting fluid (CF) is directly related to the final quality of the workpiece and is an important part of machining [1]. Globally, 2.3 billion litre of CF are used annually, 54 % of which is used for machining [2]. The life cycle evaluation of CF not only ensures the precision of machining and improves the efficiency of resource utilization, but also helps to reduce the harm to the ecological environment [3].

At present, the waste assessment technology of CF mainly relies on the experience of skilled workers, and the scheme of assessing and increasing the life cycle needs to be further developed [4]. Van Der Gast et al. used the method of the number of colonies to judge the cycle life of CF [5]. However, the process of detecting the number of bacteria is complex, expensive, and less accurate. For CF used in iron metal processing, the concentration of Fe3+ gradually increases with the increase of processing time such as milling, drilling, tapping, and boring [6]. Therefore, we speculate that testing the concentration of Fe3+ can be used as a new technology to predict the life cycle of CF. At present, there are various detection methods for Fe3+, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy [7], electrically coupled plasma mass spectrometry [8] and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopic analysis [9,10]. Among them, the advantages of the PL method are simple operation, high sensitivity, and low requirements for equipment [11].

In recent years, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) have attracted much attention due to its excellent physical and chemical properties such as low toxicity, flexible optical properties and adjustable surface structure. Qi et al. [12] synthesized N-CDs and used them to detect Fe3+ in tap water and river water. Tammina et al. [13] synthesized N-CDs and used them to detect Fe3+ in river water and circulating water samples. Khan et al. synthesized N-CDs and used them to detect Fe3+ in fluorescent based-ink and cell market. Khan et al. [14] synthesized N-CDs and used them to detect Fe3+ in sodium acetate buffer.

The composition of CF is more complex than other water environments, and further research is needed as a new detection scenario. For example, conjugated groups such as benzene ring and ketone group cause autofluorescence, which affects the sensitivity and repeatability of detection. Interestingly, the CF used for machining iron workpieces is generally not used for other metal workpieces. Therefore, monitoring of Fe3+ concentration is appropriate for evaluating the life cycle of CF. In this paper, N-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method, and the raw materials were citric acid (CA) and arginine (ARG). At the optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the PL band of the N-CDs is around 450 nm, which avoids the autofluorescence of CF at 400 nm. The N-CDs were used to detect Fe3+, the detection range is 2 to 80 μM and the detection limit is 1.2 μM. In addition, the N-CDs probe was used to detect the concentration of Fe3+ in CF that had worked for 150 and 600 hours (h), and the results were verified by Inductively Coupled Plasma Emission Spectrometer (ICP-OES).

CA, ARG, BaCl2, Na2SO4, NaCl, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, ethanol, (CH3COO)2Zn and (CH3COO)2Cd were purchased from Aladdin Industries (Shanghai, China). All chemicals are analytical reagent grade and do not require further purification before use. The pH of all solutions was controlled by phosphate buffer solution with a concentration of 0.1 M. Full synthetic CF and CF with different working hours are provided by Frank Lubrication Technology (Taizhou) Co., Ltd. CF with different working times were filtered to remove small amount of solid impurities. Ultrapure water was obtained from Millipore Direct-Q water purification system.

The morphology and microstructure of N-CDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Corporation, USA) with A1 Kα ray at a pass energy of 60.0 eV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a FTIR spectrometer (NEXUS 870). The absorption spectra were acquired on a UV spectro photometer (Varian Cary 50) and photoluminescent (PL) was detected on an Edinburgh FLS920. PL lifetimes were determined on a transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000).

N-CDs was prepared using hydrothermal method. 0.576 g of CA and 2.213 g of ARG were added to 15 ml of deionized water, and adjust the pH of the solution to 7 with formic acid. The solution was put into a hydrothermal reaction kettle and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After natural cooling, the product was centrifuged, washed with water and ethanol, and freeze-dried to obtain a viscous N-CDs probe concentrate. The yield of the product obtained is approximately 30 %.

The PL quenching curve of the N-CDs with Fe3+ was calculated according to the Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
s ν
c
where F0 and F are the PL intensities of the N-CDs without and with different concentrations of Fe3+, respectively.
K
s ν
stands for the Stern-Volmer equation constant, and c is the concentration of Fe3+. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated via the equation of
3 σ / k
, in which
σ
stands for the relative standard deviation and k is the calibration slope.

TEM was used to analyze the morphology and size of the N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the particle sizes of the N-CDs are normally distributed between 8 and 14 nm. Furthermore, the surface functional groups of N-CDs was identified by FTIR. Fig. 1b shows the FTIR spectra of the N-CDs. The specific vibrational frequency at 1174 cm−1 is attributed to the CO bond stretching vibrations in ketones [15]. The peak at 1401 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the CN bond in the amine group [16]. The broad peak around 1678 cm−1 originates from the CO and CC bond in the carboxyl and ketone groups [17]. Broad absorption bands at 3100–3600 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H [15,18].

To further confirm that N was successfully doped. Chemical elements of N-CDs were analyzed by XPS (Fig. 1c) spectroscopy and characteristic peaks at 529.5, 399.3 and 285.2 eV assigned to O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s [19], respectively. The content ratio of the three elements is O: N: C4.0: 3.0: 3.5, indicating that N-CDs have a high N content. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) has four peaks at 284.6, 286.2 and 288.2 eV, which are attributed to CC/CC, CO/CN, CO, respectively [20]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1e) shows two peaks at 399.6, 400.3 eV, attributed to CN and N-H respectively [21]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) shows two peaks at 530.5, 532.2 eV, which are attributed to CO and COH/CO, respectively [22]. The emergence of N-containing functional groups proves the successful doping of N in N-CDs, which is agreement with the FTIR results. Based on the above results, it can be inferred that the surface of the N-CDs contains a large number of hydrophilic functional groups, such as –COOH and –OH, which makes the N-CDs have excellent dispersibility in CF solution. Fig. S1 shows the stability of N-CDs. N-CDs have good batch stability and maintained good stability during 20 days of storage in an atmospheric environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between Fe3+ and the N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S2, the PL intensity of N-CDs is most sensitive to Fe3+ when the pH between 4 and 9. At highly acidic conditions, generation of positive charges on the surface of N-CDs reduces the association with the same charge Fe3+ leading to less sensitivity. The signal of N-CDs changes with pH, which may be similar to the interaction between Fe3+ and N-CDs and related to the surface structure of N-CDs. Therefore, all N-CDs detection studies control pH = 7.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs with and without Fe3+ addition. The 360 nm peak is assigned to n − π* electronic transitions due to the heteroatoms present, such as nitrogen and oxygen. After the addition of 40 μM Fe3+, the peak at 330 nm becomes weaker. This indicates the ground state adducts formation between Fe3+ and N-CDs[23,24]. The PL and excitation spectra (PLE) of N-CDs with and without Fe3+ are shown in Fig. 2b, the emission intensity of N-CDs arrives at a maximum of an excitation of 360 nm, exhibiting a deep blue PL emission at 450 nm. After the addition of Fe3+, the emission intensity of N-CDs arrives at a maximum of an excitation of 365 nm, exhibiting a blue PL emission at 455 nm. Meanwhile, the PL intensity is further decrease, which is 0.4 times that of N-CDs without Fe3+.

As shown in Fig. 2c, the FTIR spectra were used to illustrate the interaction between N-CDs and Fe3+. As the concentration of Fe3+ increases, the broad peak attributable to the CO stretching vibration becomes narrower and weaker around 1678 cm−1. This is because the coordination of Fe3+ and N-CDs makes the CO stretching vibration on the carboxyl group weakened, while the CO stretching vibration related to the ketone group remains unchanged. At the same time, the characteristic peak at 1174 cm−1 is weakened, the peak at 1360 cm−1 is broadened, and the peak at 2400 cm−1 is enhanced, which confirms the relatively strong ions binding ability of Fe3+ with N-CDs.

As shown in Fig. 2d, the time-resolved PL spectra of N-CDs with and without Fe3+. As shown in Table. 1 , the average lifetime of the N-CDs is 1.43 ns, and the average lifetime is still 1.43 ns after adding Fe3+ to the N-CDs. It can be seen that the addition of Fe3+ has no effect on the excited state N-CDs, so the quenching process can be attributed to static quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 a and Fig. S3, under the excitation of 340 nm, the autofluorescence emission peak of CF solution is at 400 nm, and the PL intensity can reach 50 % of that of N-CDs. However, at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the PL intensity of the CF solution dropped to only 8.3 % of the N-CDs. Fig. 3b displays the PL spectra of N-CDs in CF at different excitation wavelengths between 310 nm and 370 nm. When the N-CDs are excited by light with a wavelength of 360 nm, the PL intensity is the strongest, and the emission peak is at 447 nm. As the excitation wavelength increases from 310 nm to 360 nm, the PL intensity of the N-CDs increases slowly, and the emission peak slowly red shifts from 427 nm to 447 nm. When the excitation wavelength exceeds 360 nm, the PL intensity of the N-CDs begins to decrease and further red shift. It can be seen that the main factors that regulate the PL signal of N-CDs are as follows: First, control the pH and conduct detection research at the optimal pH. Second, control the optimal excitation wavelength to achieve detection research. Third, avoid the autofluorescence of CF.

To explore the sensitivity of N-CDs to Fe3+ in CF, titration was used to determine the linear response range and limit of Fe3+ concentration detection. In order to determine the concentration of N-CDs and its complexation reaction time with Fe3+, 40 μM Fe3+ was added to 10 μM N-CDs and mixed thoroughly. As shown in Fig. S4, it can be seen that N-CDs can be complexed when mixed with Fe3+, and the PL intensity will not change with time. At the same time, the use of 10 μM N-CDs can achieve good response to low concentrations of Fe3+.

As shown in Fig. 3c, a gradual quenching of the PL from N-CDs is observed on addition of Fe3+ in the concentration range 10 μM to 80 μM. As shown in Fig. 3d, when the concentration of Fe3+ is between 10 μM and 80 μM, the linear response formula is
F -
F
0
/
F
0
= 0.05649 + 0.0561 ×
F
e
3 +
, and the correlation coefficient is R2 = 0.9623. Here, F0 represents the PL intensity of N-CD sat different concentrations of Fe3+, and F represents the PL intensity of N-CDs without Fe3+ in CF. ICP-OES and PL tests were performed on CFs processed for 150 and 600 h of workpiece respectively. The test results of Fe3+ concentration in ICP-OES were used as the abscissa, and the results of (F-F0)/F0 in the PL spectrum were used as the ordinate. The results show that the two points of CF of 150 and 600 h can match the above standard curve. Compared with other detection methods for Fe3+ (Table. 2 ), the N-CDs were used in a new detection environment, and had the advantages of wide detection range and low detection limit.

In practical application, CF is usually used for processing alloy work pieces, so CF will inevitably be mixed with some other metal cations and anionic. In order to explore the selectivity of N-CDs to metal ions in CF, different interference ions were added during Fe3+ detection. As shown in Fig. 3e, the orange column indicates the luminescence intensity of N-CDs after adding interfering metal ions, and the green column indicates the luminescence intensity of N-CDs after adding 40 μM Fe3+ to the solution containing interfering ions. The results showed that the stability and selectivity of N-CDs probes were good in the interference environment.

In this paper, CA and ARG were used as N and C source, and a kind of high N containing N-CDs was synthesized for the detection of Fe3+ concentration in CF. The N-CDs probes have an average size of 11 nm and exhibit good dispersion in CF. Time-resolved PL spectra show that the PL quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ belongs to the static quenching effect. At the same time, the spectral detection technology of N-CDs avoids the autofluorescence of CF. In the range of Fe3+ concentration from 2 to 80 μM, the PL intensity of N-CDs varied linearly with Fe3+ concentration, and the detection limit is 1.2 μM. This study provides a new idea for the detection application of N-CDs in complex environments and the life cycle assessment application of CF.

Jiadi Lian: Writing – review & editing, Software, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Bifeng Chen: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Qing Han: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation. Hangqing Xie: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Software, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Jing Xu: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research is financially supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No.52275182, No.12304459), the National Nature Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province, China (No. LTGS23E060002), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang, China (No. GK229909299001-14), the Tribology Science Fund of State Key Laboratory of tribology in Advanced Equipment (No.SKLTKF22B02), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No.LQ21E030005) and the Fcundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang (No.2021YW44).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110677.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
